---
id: 001-03
status: ready
depends: [001-02]
gate: { passed: 2026-07-16T21:53:36Z, brief: 9578a10934af0854, overrides: [] }

# Drawer chat & proposal widgets

## Goal

The card drawer holds a live chat: the artifact under construction on top, the conversation below,
tool calls as collapsed one-liners, and every proposal rendered as an **accept / edit / reject**
widget (`.knowledge/product/features/define-refine.md`). This is the pure-UI slice over stories
001-01 and 001-02.

## Approach

- Extend `src/app/components/card-drawer.tsx` (or split it) with a chat pane: message composer,
  assistant output rendered incrementally from the `stream_event` deltas 001-01 forwards over the
  WS session channel, tool-call events as collapsed expandable one-liners.
- The chat pane binds to a session id plus its card, never to a kind: it renders whatever
  session the frontmatter (or a dev entry) names. Until 001-04/05 wire the real entries and
  seeds, a dev-only entry spawns a `define` session on the story's epic through the 001-01
  routes — the session whose `propose_stories` calls make the mini-card widgets demoable here.
- A widget component per proposal shape: proposed epics and stories render as mini-cards with
  per-item accept/edit/reject plus accept-all; `ask_user` renders quick-reply chips with a
  free-text fallback. Resolution calls 001-02's per-item endpoint (accepted items write once,
  edit/reject texts batch into one resume); the board updates over the existing board channel,
  no reload.
- Edit opens the proposal payload inline; submitting sends it back as that item's edit outcome,
  resuming the session for a re-proposal — accepting is the only write, per the spec
  (`.knowledge/product/features/define-refine.md` §Proposal widgets).
- The artifact pane is a slot the chat stories fill (Decisions checklist for shape, the brief for
  refine); this story ships the layout and the widget machinery with the shape/define widgets as
  the first concrete case.
- Client state additions in `src/app/lib/` (session events, pending proposals) following the
  existing `board-store.ts` pattern.

## Blast radius

`src/app/` only: `card-drawer.tsx` and new chat/widget components, plus `lib/api.ts` and a session
store in `lib/`. Possibly small additions to `src/shared/channels.ts` types if the UI needs
shapes the server stories did not export.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A message sent from the drawer streams the assistant's reply into the chat pane as it
      generates.
- [ ] A `propose_stories` call renders mini-cards with per-card accept/edit/reject and
      accept-all; accepting lands the cards on the board without a reload.
- [ ] Editing a proposal resumes the session with the edited payload, and the following
      re-proposal reflects the edit.
- [ ] An `ask_user` question renders quick-reply chips plus free text, and answering resumes the
      conversation in place.
- [ ] Tool calls appear as collapsed one-liners that expand on click and never interleave with
      prose as noise.

## Out of scope

- The chat entry points and seeds (`n`, `r`, header shaping entry; 001-04 and 001-05).
- The brief artifact pane content (001-05) and the gating badge (001-06).
- Mobile layout polish (v2, `.knowledge/product/features/mobile.md`).

## Open questions

None.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- reviewer: Review code for quality, security, and performance. Use PROACTIVELY after implementing features or before merging. Runs project checks automatically. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- ui-ux-reviewer: Design and review UI/UX using Chrome MCP. Creates designs for new features and audits existing UI for visual consistency, accessibility, and UX quality. Use PROACTIVELY for design work, after UI changes, or before releases. (Tools: All tools)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# Implementation plan: 001-02 board tools (in-process MCP server & proposals)
2	
3	Technical plan for `.helm/board/epics/001-session-foundation/02-board-tools.md`. Every structural
4	decision the story's Approach leaves open is settled here; implementation is mechanical execution.
5	Library facts below were re-verified against current docs (context7 / npm) on 2026-07-17.
6	
7	## Decisions the Approach left open
8	
9	1. **Stack raw-mount shape.** `ServiceContext` gains `http: ServiceHttp` with `port` and
10	   `mount(prefix, handler)`, where `handler` is fetch-style (`(request: Request) => Response |
11	   Promise<Response>`). A dispatcher middleware in `create-node-server.ts`, registered after `/ws`
12	   and before the worker mounts, routes by longest matching prefix. Fetch-style keeps Hono out of
13	   the consumer-facing service surface; `port` is required because the spawned CLI must reach the
14	   node server directly (never through the vite dev proxy).
15	2. **Transport library.** `@hono/mcp` (v0.3.1, the honojs middleware package) provides
16	   `StreamableHTTPTransport`, whose `handleRequest(c)` takes a Hono context and returns a fetch
17	   `Response`. Its peers match what Helm already has: `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` ^1.29.0 (the
18	   spike-verified version, still latest) and zod 4 (the stack's `z`). Helm builds a small Hono
19	   sub-app for `/mcp/:token` and mounts its `fetch` via `ctx.http.mount`. Fresh `McpServer` plus
20	   fresh transport per request, the spike-verified stateless pattern.
21	3. **Module layout.** `src/server/mcp/` holds `registry.ts` (spawn tokens + port), `schemas.ts`
22	   (pure-zod payload and wire schemas, "next to the tools", importable from the browser bundle),
23	   `tools.ts` (tool table + handlers), `server.ts` (per-request `McpServer` construction). Two new
24	   services: `src/server/services/mcp.ts` (the HTTP mount) and `src/server/services/proposals.ts`
25	   (store, `proposal` WS channel, resolution, deferred resumes). Import graph is acyclic:
26	   sessions → mcp/registry; mcp service → mcp/server → mcp/tools → proposals service → sessions
27	   service.
28	4. **Proposal model.** A tool call records one `Proposal` with `items[]`. `propose_epics` and
29	   `propose_stories` are multi-item (one item per epic/story, per-item resolution as define-refine
30	   specifies); `update_brief`, `resolve_question`, `raise_decision`, `flag_risk` are single-item.
31	   `ask_user` is not a proposal: it records a separate `Question` (answer lifecycle, not
32	   accept/edit/reject).
33	5. **Questions and proposals share one WS channel.** New `proposal` channel with a single server
34	   message `snapshot: { proposals, questions }`, sent on subscribe and rebroadcast on every change
35	   (the board-channel pattern; the pending set is small and a missed frame is irrelevant).
36	6. **Resolution semantics.** One RPC call per item. Accept and edit perform the item's board write
37	   inside the write queue before the item is marked resolved (a failed write leaves it pending and
38	   the RPC errors). When the last item resolves and any item was edited or rejected, the outcomes
39	   batch into one resume message; an all-accepted proposal triggers no resume. If the session is
40	   mid-turn at that moment, the resume is held and flushed on the session's `closed` event.
41	7. **Edit outcome.** An edit carries a full replacement payload (validated against the tool's item
42	   schema in the handler) plus an optional note; the edited payload is written like an accept and
43	   the edit is relayed in the resume message.
44	8. **Recorded-only tools.** `raise_decision`: accept returns `UNSUPPORTED_RESOLUTION` (its write
45	   lands with 001-04); edit/reject take the generic resume path. `flag_risk`: every outcome
46	   returns `UNSUPPORTED_RESOLUTION` (its resolution semantics land with 001-06, and the adversary
47	   is never resumed). Both record, validate, and broadcast normally.
48	9. **Ordinal minting.** `src/board/ordinals.ts` mints one above the highest ordinal ever used:
49	   max over the live tree and `git log --diff-filter=A --no-renames --name-only` over
50	   `.helm/board/epics/` (`--no-renames` so a renamed path still counts as an add). Stories mint the
51	   same way, scoped to `<NNN>-*/` directories of the target epic, because board-storage retires
52	   story ordinals too. Minting runs inside the same write-queue task as the write. A git failure
53	   throws (managed repos are git repos; falling back silently could reuse an ordinal).
54	10. **Target epic for shape's `propose_stories`.** The payload names the epic by `slug` (required
55	    for `shape`, forbidden for `define`, whose session is bound to its epic). A slug is content,
56	    not identity, so "tool payloads never carry ids" holds literally; the server resolves slug →
57	    epic and errors on zero or multiple matches, at call time and again at accept time.
58	11. **`depends` in story drafts** are sibling slugs. Accept resolves each against (a) items of the
59	    same proposal already accepted into this epic, then (b) existing stories in the target epic;
60	    an unresolvable reference fails the accept with `BAD_REQUEST` naming the missing sibling, so
61	    acceptance order follows dependency order and nothing is dropped silently.
62	12. **Generated bodies are hardcoded builders** in `src/board/markdown.ts` (story body with the six
63	    template headings, epic body with title + goal + rationale). The template override machinery
64	    (`.helm/templates/`) is separate future work; templates.md already records the model.
65	13. **`ask_user` goes to every spawnable kind**, including the cold ones (`research`,
66	    `adversary`), per the story. Answering a cold kind's question fails with the existing
67	    `SESSION_COLD`; acceptable until 001-06 reworks the adversary flow. Noted, not hidden.
68	14. **Kind → board-tool names** live in `src/sessions/kinds.ts` (`boardTools` on `KindRow`), names
69	    only; the server-side tool table is `Record<BoardToolName, …>` so the compiler enforces every
70	    name has a definition.
71	
72	## Part A: stack extension (`../stack`, plugin-node)
73	
74	Follow the stack's own playbooks (`conventions.md`, `plugin-authoring.md`, `testing.md`) when
75	implementing; `pnpm check && pnpm test` in `../stack` gates the change.
76	
77	### `plugins/node/src/server/service.ts`
78	
79	```ts
80	export interface ServiceHttp {
81		// The port the node server listens on (fixed from config before listen).
82		port: number;
83		// Route every request whose path equals `prefix` or starts with
84		// `prefix + "/"` to `handler`. Longest registered prefix wins; an exact
85		// duplicate prefix throws. Paths are not rewritten.
86		mount(
87			prefix: string,
88			handler: (request: Request) => Response | Promise<Response>,
89		): void;
90	}
91	
92	export interface ServiceContext {
93		log: ServiceLogger;
94		ws: WsHub;
95		http: ServiceHttp;
96	}
97	```
98	
99	### `plugins/node/src/server/create-node-server.ts`
100	
101	- `const mounts = new Map<string, (request: Request) => Response | Promise<Response>>()`.
102	- `mount(prefix, handler)`: validate `prefix` starts with `/`, has no trailing slash, is not `/`;
103	  throw on duplicate key.
104	- Dispatcher middleware registered immediately after the `/ws` route (so `/ws` always wins and
105	  mounts precede worker/static/SPA):
106	
107	```ts
108	app.use("*", async (c, next) => {
109		const handler = matchMount(c.req.path); // longest prefix: path === p || path.startsWith(p + "/")
110		if (handler) return handler(c.req.raw);
111		await next();
112	});
113	```
114	
115	- `startServices` passes `{ log, ws: hub, http: { port, mount } }`.
116	- A consumer mounting a worker prefix (`/rpc`) would shadow it; document, don't guard.
117	
118	### Tests (`create-node-server.test.ts`, follow existing style)
119	
120	- A service's mounted handler receives GET and POST under its prefix (`/mcp` and `/mcp/abc/def`),
121	  and the request path is unrewritten.
122	- `/mcpx` falls through past a `/mcp` mount (SPA/404 behavior unchanged).
123	- Longest prefix wins when two mounts nest; duplicate prefix throws; invalid prefix throws.
124	- `ctx.http.port` equals the configured port.
125	
126	### Docs
127	
128	`.knowledge/architecture/runtime.md` §Node target: extend the service sentence to
129	`{ log, ws, http }` and one line on `http.mount`/`http.port`.
130	
131	## Part B: Helm dependencies
132	
133	`package.json` additions (then `pnpm install`):
134	
135	```
136	"@hono/mcp": "^0.3.1",
137	"@modelcontextprotocol/sdk": "^1.29.0",
138	"hono": "^4.12.23"        // @hono/mcp peer; matches the stack's version
139	```
140	
141	## Part C: session kinds and runner
142	
143	### `src/sessions/kinds.ts`
144	
145	```ts
146	export const BOARD_TOOLS = [
147		"propose_epics",
148		"propose_stories",
149		"raise_decision",
150		"update_brief",
151		"resolve_question",
152		"flag_risk",
153		"ask_user",
154	] as const;
155	export const boardToolNameSchema = z.enum(BOARD_TOOLS);
156	export type BoardToolName = z.infer<typeof boardToolNameSchema>;
157	
158	export const MCP_SERVER_NAME = "helm"; // tool ids on the CLI: mcp__helm__<tool>
159	```

165	| --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
166	| init      | `["ask_user"]` (`propose_scaffold` is v2)                    |

214	## Part D: MCP module (`src/server/mcp/`)
215	
216	### `registry.ts` (spawn tokens + endpoint URLs)
217	
218	```ts
219	import type { Attach } from "../services/sessions.ts"; // export Attach there (type-only import)
220	
221	export interface SpawnBinding {
222		kind: SessionKind;
223		attach?: Attach;
224		sessionId?: string; // set once system/init reports it
225	}
226	
227	const bindings = new Map<string, SpawnBinding>();
228	let port: number | undefined;
229	
230	export function setMcpPort(p: number): void;
231	export function mcpEndpointUrl(token: string): string; // `http://127.0.0.1:${port}/mcp/${token}`; throws if port unset
232	export function registerSpawn(token: string, binding: { kind: SessionKind; attach?: Attach }): void;
233	export function bindSessionId(token: string, sessionId: string): void;
234	export function releaseSpawn(token: string): void;
235	export function lookupSpawn(token: string): SpawnBinding | undefined;
236	```
237	
238	### `schemas.ts` (pure zod; no node imports — the SPA bundle reaches it via channels.ts)
239	
240	```ts
241	const slugSchema = z
242		.string()
243		.regex(/^[a-z0-9]+(?:-[a-z0-9]+)*$/, "slugs are lowercase words joined by hyphens")
244		.max(50);
245	
246	export const storyDraftSchema = z.object({
247		slug: slugSchema,
248		title: z.string().min(1),
249		goal: z.string().min(1),
250		depends: z.array(slugSchema).default([]), // sibling slugs, resolved at accept
251	});
252	export type StoryDraft = z.infer<typeof storyDraftSchema>;
253	
254	export const epicDraftSchema = z.object({
255		slug: slugSchema,
256		title: z.string().min(1),
257		goal: z.string().min(1),
258		rationale: z.string().optional(),
259		stories: z.array(storyDraftSchema).default([]), // a shaping proposal may carry draft stories
260	});
261	export type EpicDraft = z.infer<typeof epicDraftSchema>;
262	
263	export const proposeEpicsPayloadSchema = z.object({ epics: z.array(epicDraftSchema).min(1) });
264	// Two variants of propose_stories: shape names the target epic by slug,
265	// define is bound to its epic and must not name one.
266	export const proposeStoriesShapePayloadSchema = z.object({
267		epic: slugSchema,
268		stories: z.array(storyDraftSchema).min(1),
269	});
270	export const proposeStoriesDefinePayloadSchema = z.object({
271		stories: z.array(storyDraftSchema).min(1),
272	});
273	export const updateBriefPayloadSchema = z.object({
274		section: z.enum(BRIEF_SECTIONS),
275		content: z.string().min(1),
276	});
277	export const resolveQuestionPayloadSchema = z.object({
278		question: z.string().min(1), // exact text of an unchecked Open questions item
279		answer: z.string().min(1),
280	});
281	export const raiseDecisionPayloadSchema = z.object({
282		decision: z.string().min(1),
283		context: z.string().optional(),
284		settledBy: z.enum(["human", "research"]),
285	});
286	export const flagRiskPayloadSchema = z.object({
287		title: z.string().min(1),
288		detail: z.string().min(1),
289	});
290	export const askUserPayloadSchema = z.object({
291		question: z.string().min(1),
292		recommendation: z.string().min(1), // the grilling contract: always carry a recommended answer
293		options: z.array(z.string().min(1)).max(6).optional(),
294	});
295	```
296	
297	Wire shapes (same file):
298	
299	```ts
300	export const proposalResolutionSchema = z.discriminatedUnion("type", [
301		z.object({ type: z.literal("accept") }),
302		z.object({ type: z.literal("edit"), payload: z.unknown(), note: z.string().optional() }),
303		z.object({ type: z.literal("reject"), reason: z.string().min(1) }),
304	]);
305	export type ProposalResolution = z.infer<typeof proposalResolutionSchema>;
306	
307	const proposalBase = {
308		id: z.uuid(),
309		sessionId: z.uuid(),
310		kind: sessionKindSchema,
311		createdAt: z.iso.datetime(),
312	};
313	function itemsOf<S extends z.ZodType>(payload: S) {
314		return z.array(z.object({ payload, resolution: proposalResolutionSchema.optional() })).min(1);
315	}
316	export const proposalSchema = z.discriminatedUnion("tool", [
317		z.object({ ...proposalBase, tool: z.literal("propose_epics"), items: itemsOf(epicDraftSchema) }),
318		z.object({
319			...proposalBase,
320			tool: z.literal("propose_stories"),
321			epic: slugSchema.optional(), // present when a shape session named the target
322			items: itemsOf(storyDraftSchema),
323		}),
324		z.object({ ...proposalBase, tool: z.literal("update_brief"), items: itemsOf(updateBriefPayloadSchema) }),
325		z.object({ ...proposalBase, tool: z.literal("resolve_question"), items: itemsOf(resolveQuestionPayloadSchema) }),
326		z.object({ ...proposalBase, tool: z.literal("raise_decision"), items: itemsOf(raiseDecisionPayloadSchema) }),
327		z.object({ ...proposalBase, tool: z.literal("flag_risk"), items: itemsOf(flagRiskPayloadSchema) }),
328	]);
329	export type Proposal = z.infer<typeof proposalSchema>;
330	
331	export const questionSchema = z.object({
332		id: z.uuid(),
333		sessionId: z.uuid(),
334		kind: sessionKindSchema,
335		createdAt: z.iso.datetime(),
336		...askUserPayloadSchema.shape,
337	});
338	export type Question = z.infer<typeof questionSchema>;
339	
340	export const proposalSnapshotSchema = z.object({
341		proposals: z.array(proposalSchema),
342		questions: z.array(questionSchema),
343	});
344	export type ProposalSnapshot = z.infer<typeof proposalSnapshotSchema>;
345	```
346	
347	Note the multi-item tools store one draft per item (`items[].payload` is a single `EpicDraft` /
348	`StoryDraft`): the tool call's array payload is split into items at record time, so per-item
349	resolution needs no sub-indexing.
350	
351	### `tools.ts` (tool table + handlers)
352	
353	```ts
354	interface ToolDefinition {
355		description: string;
356		// SDK 1.x takes a zod raw shape (spike-verified with zod 4).
357		inputShape(kind: SessionKind): z.ZodRawShape;
358		handle(binding: SpawnBinding, args: unknown): Promise<CallToolResult>;
359	}
360	export const TOOL_TABLE: Record<BoardToolName, ToolDefinition>;
361	```
362	
363	- `inputShape` is `.shape` of the payload schema; `propose_stories` returns the shape/define
364	  variant by `kind`.
365	- Every `handle` re-parses `args` with the full payload schema (`safeParse`; the SDK already
366	  validated, this is the trusted-type boundary), runs call-time semantic checks, then records via
367	  the proposals service and returns a text result.
368	- Call-time semantic checks (return `isError: true` text on failure, nothing recorded):
369	  - all tools: `binding.sessionId` set (unreachable in practice: tool calls follow `system/init`).
370	  - `propose_stories` (shape): payload `epic` slug resolves to exactly one live epic.
371	  - `propose_stories` (define) / `update_brief` / `resolve_question`: `binding.attach` present with
372	    the right type (spawn wiring guarantees it; check anyway, cheap).
373	  - `resolve_question`: the bound story currently has an unchecked Open questions item exactly
374	    matching `question` (via `boardSnapshot()`), so the model gets immediate feedback on a typo.
375	- Success result texts (the model-facing contract):
376	  - proposal tools: `Recorded proposal <id> with <n> item(s). The user will accept, edit, or
377	    reject each item; continue, or end your turn and await the outcome.`
378	  - `raise_decision` / `flag_risk`: `Recorded. The user will resolve it; continue or end your turn.`
379	  - `ask_user`: `Question recorded. End your turn now; the user's answer arrives as the next
380	    message.`
381	- Tool descriptions (registered with the SDK, model-facing):
382	  - `propose_epics`: "Propose one or more epics (optionally with draft stories). Each epic renders
383	    as a card the user accepts, edits, or rejects; accepting writes it to the board."
384	  - `propose_stories`: "Propose stories{ for the epic named by slug | for this epic}. Each story
385	    renders as a card the user resolves individually."
386	  - `update_brief`: "Propose replacing one section of this story's brief."
387	  - `resolve_question`: "Propose resolving one of this story's open questions with an answer."
388	  - `raise_decision`: "Raise a feature-level decision that must be settled before breakdown, tagged
389	    by who can settle it."
390	  - `flag_risk`: "Raise a blocking flaw in the brief: name where an implementer would stumble."
391	  - `ask_user`: "Ask the user one question, with your recommended answer and optional quick-reply
392	    options. End your turn after calling."
393	
394	### `server.ts` (per-request construction)
395	
396	```ts
397	export function buildMcpServer(binding: SpawnBinding): McpServer {
398		const mcp = new McpServer({ name: MCP_SERVER_NAME, version: "0.0.0" });
399		for (const name of KIND_REGISTRY[binding.kind].boardTools ?? []) {
400			const def = TOOL_TABLE[name];
401			mcp.registerTool(
402				name,
403				{ description: def.description, inputSchema: def.inputShape(binding.kind) },
404				(args) => def.handle(binding, args),
405			);
406		}
407		return mcp;
408	}
409	```

411	## Part E: services
412	
413	### `src/server/services/mcp.ts`
414	
415	```ts
416	import { Hono } from "hono";
417	import { StreamableHTTPTransport } from "@hono/mcp";
418	
419	export default defineService({
420		name: "mcp",
421		start: (ctx) => {
422			setMcpPort(ctx.http.port);
423			const app = new Hono();
424			app.all("/mcp/:token", async (c) => {
425				const binding = lookupSpawn(c.req.param("token"));
426				if (binding === undefined) return c.text("unknown MCP token", 404);
427				const transport = new StreamableHTTPTransport();
428				await buildMcpServer(binding).connect(transport);
429				return transport.handleRequest(c);
430			});
431			ctx.http.mount("/mcp", (request) => app.fetch(request));
432		},
433	});
434	```
435	
436	Fresh `McpServer` + transport per request (stateless, one transport per server instance), matching
437	the spike; the closure over `binding` is what binds every tool call server-side to its session and
438	card.
439	
440	### `src/server/services/proposals.ts`
441	
442	State (module singletons, in-memory only; a pending proposal dies with the process, per Out of
443	scope):
444	
445	```ts
446	const proposals = new Map<string, Proposal>();
447	const questions = new Map<string, Question>();
448	const heldResumes = new Map<string, string[]>(); // sessionId -> resume messages awaiting `closed`
449	let handle: ChannelHandle<(typeof proposalChannel)["server"]> | undefined;
450	```
451	
452	Recording (called from tool handlers; each broadcasts a fresh snapshot):
453	
454	```ts
455	export function recordProposal(
456		binding: SpawnBinding & { sessionId: string },
457		tool: Exclude<BoardToolName, "ask_user">,
458		items: unknown[],           // already schema-parsed payload drafts
459		epic?: string,              // shape propose_stories target slug
460	): Proposal;
461	export function recordQuestion(
462		binding: SpawnBinding & { sessionId: string },
463		payload: AskUserPayload,
464	): Question;
465	```
466	
467	Resolution (called from routes):
468	
469	```ts
470	export async function resolveProposalItem(input: {
471		proposalId: string;
472		item: number;               // index into items
473		resolution: ProposalResolution; // edit.payload validated against the tool's item schema here
474	}): Promise<void>;
475	export async function answerQuestion(input: { questionId: string; answer: string }): Promise<void>;
476	```
477	
478	`resolveProposalItem` flow:
479	
480	1. Look up proposal + item: unknown id/index → `NOT_FOUND`; `item.resolution` already set →
481	   `PROPOSAL_RESOLVED` (409).
482	2. Tool gate: `flag_risk` any outcome, `raise_decision` accept → `UNSUPPORTED_RESOLUTION` (501).
483	3. `edit`: parse `resolution.payload` with the tool's item schema (`epicDraftSchema`,
484	   `storyDraftSchema`, …); failure → `BAD_REQUEST` with the prettified zod error. The parsed
485	   payload replaces the item's payload.
486	4. `accept`/`edit`: perform the board write (below) inside `enqueueWrite`; a throw propagates to
487	   the RPC and leaves the item unresolved.
488	5. Mark the item resolved, broadcast a snapshot.
489	6. If every item is resolved: delete the proposal (broadcast again); if any item was edited or
490	   rejected, compose `proposalOutcomePrompt` and dispatch it (below).
491	
492	Board writes per tool (all inside the queue task, minting included):
493	
494	- `propose_epics` item: `nextEpicOrdinal` → `createEpic` (epic dir + `epic.md` + one story file per
495	  draft story, ordinals `01…N` in payload order, `depends` resolved among the same item's drafts;
496	  unresolvable → `BAD_REQUEST`).
497	- `propose_stories` item: resolve the target epic (bound card for define, payload slug for shape;
498	  vanished/ambiguous → `NOT_FOUND`/`BAD_REQUEST`) → `nextStoryOrdinal` → `createStory`; `depends`
499	  resolved against earlier-accepted items of this proposal (tracked as `slug → minted id` on the
500	  in-memory proposal) then existing stories in the epic; unresolvable → `BAD_REQUEST`.
501	- `update_brief` item: fresh `readStoryFile` of the bound story (ENOENT → `NOT_FOUND`, invalid →
502	  `INVALID_FILE`, mirroring `story.move`) → `replaceBriefSection` → `writeStory`.
503	- `resolve_question` item: fresh read → `checkQuestion`; no matching unchecked item →
504	  `NOT_FOUND` → `writeStory`.
505	
506	`answerQuestion` flow: unknown id → `NOT_FOUND`; delete + broadcast; dispatch
507	`questionAnswerPrompt(question, answer)`.
508	
509	Resume dispatch:
510	
511	```ts
512	async function dispatchResume(sessionId: string, message: string): Promise<void> {
513		if (isSessionLive(sessionId)) {
514			heldResumes.set(sessionId, [...(heldResumes.get(sessionId) ?? []), message]);
515			return;
516		}
517		await messageSession({ sessionId, prompt: message }); // SESSION_COLD/SESSION_STALE propagate to the RPC
518	}
519	```
520	
521	Service `start`: register the `proposal` channel (`onSubscribe` sends the current snapshot),
522	subscribe `onSessionClosed` (new hook, below) to flush `heldResumes` for that session (join the
523	messages with a blank line, one `messageSession` call; log failures and drop, the session channel's
524	`closed` frame already told the client the turn ended). Stop handler clears all three maps.
525

551	## Part F: board module additions
552	
553	### `src/board/ordinals.ts`
554	
555	```ts
556	import { execFile } from "node:child_process"; // promisified
557	
558	// Highest epic ordinal ever used: live dirs under .helm/board/epics plus every
559	// path git ever recorded as added there. --no-renames keeps a renamed dir
560	// counted as an add; --format= suppresses commit headers.
561	export async function nextEpicOrdinal(repoPath: string): Promise<number>;
562	export async function nextStoryOrdinal(repoPath: string, epicId: string): Promise<number>;
563	```
564	
565	- Git invocation: `git -C <repoPath> log --diff-filter=A --no-renames --name-only --format= -- .helm/board/epics`.
566	  Parse lines with `/^\.helm\/board\/epics\/(\d{3})-/` (epics) and
567	  `/^\.helm\/board\/epics\/(\d{3})-[^/]+\/(\d{2})-[^/]+\.md$/` filtered to `epicId` (stories; the
568	  epic is matched by ordinal, not slug, so a renamed epic dir still retires its stories'
569	  ordinals). Empty output (fresh repo, nothing committed) is fine.
570	- Live side: `readdir` + `EPIC_DIR_RE` / `STORY_FILE_RE` (ENOENT → empty).
571	- Return `max + 1` (so `1` on a virgin board). Callers format with `padStart(3|2, "0")`; three
572	  digits cap at 999 epics / 99 stories, throw a plain error past that.
573

578	export interface EpicSeed { slug: string; title: string; goal: string; rationale?: string }

591		ordinal: number,

618	`src/shared/channels.ts`:
619

621	import { proposalSnapshotSchema } from "../server/mcp/schemas.ts"; // pure zod, browser-safe
622	
623	// Pending proposals and questions: full snapshot on (re)subscribe and on every
624	// change, like the board channel. Resolutions go over RPC.

631	## Part H: routes (`src/worker/routes/proposal.ts`)
632	
633	```ts
634	export const proposal = {
635		// Per-item resolution; the proposal channel's snapshot is the authority, so
636		// nothing is returned. Board writes serialize through the write queue; a
637		// fully resolved proposal with edits/rejections resumes the session (held
638		// until its turn ends if it is mid-turn).
639		resolve: procedure()
640			.input(z.object({
641				proposalId: z.uuid(),
642				item: z.number().int().nonnegative(),
643				resolution: proposalResolutionSchema,
644			}))
645			.handler(({ input }) => resolveProposalItem(input)),
646		// Answers a pending ask_user question and resumes the session with it.
647		answer: procedure()
648			.input(z.object({ questionId: z.uuid(), answer: z.string().min(1) }))
649			.handler(({ input }) => answerQuestion(input)),
650	};
651	```
652	
653	Run `pnpm generate` to regenerate `src/worker/routes/index.ts` and the services barrel
654	(`src/server/services/index.ts`) after adding the new files.

657	
658	- `.knowledge/architecture/api.md`: add `proposal.resolve` and `proposal.answer` to the procedures
659	  table; document the `proposal` WS channel (snapshot semantics) next to `board`/`session`; add
660	  error rows:
661	
662	| Code                     | Meaning                                                          | `data` |
663	| ------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ------ |
664	| `PROPOSAL_RESOLVED`      | The item is already resolved / the question already answered (HTTP 409). | none |
665	| `UNSUPPORTED_RESOLUTION` | The tool's accept path lands with a later stage (`raise_decision` 001-04, `flag_risk` 001-06) (HTTP 501). | none |
666	
667	- `.knowledge/architecture/claude-integration.md` §Board tools: one sentence that caller identity
668	  rides the per-spawn endpoint (`/mcp/<token>`), so payloads never carry ids.
669	- `.knowledge/architecture/board-storage.md` §Layout: one sentence that minting scans the live
670	  tree plus git's added-path history, which is what retires ordinals permanently.

672	## Edge cases (behavior settled here)
673	
674	- Unknown `/mcp/<token>` → 404; the CLI reports the server as failed in `system/init`.
675	- Payload fails SDK schema validation → the SDK returns `isError: true` without running the
676	  handler; nothing recorded (acceptance criterion 6). Semantic failures inside the handler return
677	  `isError: true` with a corrective message.
678	- Resolving an item twice (double-click, two clients) → second call gets `PROPOSAL_RESOLVED`.
679	- Accept whose board write throws → RPC surfaces the error, item stays pending, retry works.
680	- Reject/edit finishing a proposal while the session is still streaming → resume held, flushed on
681	  `closed`; multiple held messages join into one resume.
682	- Answering a cold kind's `ask_user` (`research`, `adversary`) → `SESSION_COLD` from
683	  `messageSession`, surfaced to the client; reworked in 001-06.
684	- Session reseeded between record and resume → `messageSession` already reseeds from the card; the
685	  resume message rides the fresh session.
686	- Orchestrator restart → pending proposals and questions vanish (stated out of scope); the chat
687	  resumes and re-proposes.
688	- Epic slug duplicated across epics: legal on disk; only shape's `propose_stories` needs
689	  slug → epic resolution and errors on ambiguity.
690	- `.helm/board/epics` with no git history (nothing committed yet) → minting falls back to the live
691	  tree alone; a repo where `git log` itself fails throws.
692	- Two accepts racing → both queue tasks run serially; each mints inside its task, so ordinals never
693	  collide.
694	- Watcher pickup: `createEpic`/`createStory` write into the watched tree; `addDir` + `add` events
695	  rebuild the snapshot (acceptance criterion 3 needs no extra wiring).
696	
697	## Implementation order
698	
699	1. Stack: `ServiceHttp` + dispatcher + tests + `runtime.md` (in `../stack`; `pnpm check`,
700	   `pnpm test`).
701	2. Helm deps (`@hono/mcp`, `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, `hono`), `pnpm install`.
702	3. `src/sessions/kinds.ts` (board tool names, prompts), `src/sessions/runner.ts` (`mcpUrl`),
703	   `src/sessions/prompts.ts` (resume builders).
704	4. `src/server/mcp/schemas.ts`, `src/shared/channels.ts`.
705	5. `src/board/ordinals.ts`, `src/board/create.ts`, `markdown.ts` builders.
706	6. `src/server/mcp/registry.ts`; `src/server/services/sessions.ts` wiring (`Attach` export, token
707	   lifecycle, `onSessionClosed`, `isSessionLive`).
708	7. `src/server/services/proposals.ts`, `src/server/mcp/tools.ts`, `src/server/mcp/server.ts`,
709	   `src/server/services/mcp.ts`.
710	8. `src/worker/routes/proposal.ts`, `pnpm generate`.
711	9. Knowledge-base updates (Part I).
712	10. `pnpm check`; verification below.
713	
714	## Verification (no test suite in Helm; drive the real thing)
715	
716	Setup: scratch target repo (`git init`, empty `.helm/board/epics/`), `helm.config.json` pointing at
717	it, `stack dev` (node server on 8788). Drive RPC with `curl` against `/rpc` and watch WS with a
718	scratch `ws` script (subscribe to `board`, `session`, `proposal`).
719	
720	Per acceptance criterion:
721	
722	1. `session.spawn { kind: "shape", prompt: … }`; assert the `system/init` event shows
723	   `mcp_servers: [{ name: "helm", status: "connected" }]` only, and `tools` contains exactly the
724	   kind's `mcp__helm__*` names.
725	2. Spawn with a prompt that forces one `propose_epics` call; assert a `proposal` snapshot arrives
726	   with the pending proposal and `git -C <repo> status --porcelain` shows nothing under
727	   `.helm/board/`.
728	3. `proposal.resolve { …, resolution: { type: "accept" } }`; assert the epic dir + `epic.md`
729	   exist, and a `board` snapshot containing the epic arrives via the watcher without restart.
730	4. Repeat with `{ type: "reject", reason: … }`; assert the session resumes (new `system/init` on
731	   the session channel with the same session id) and the next assistant text addresses the reason.
732	5. Force an `ask_user` call; assert the question appears in the `proposal` snapshot and the
733	   process exits (`closed` frame, `result` success); `proposal.answer`; assert the resumed turn
734	   uses the answer.
735	6. `curl` the live `/mcp/<token>` endpoint directly with a JSON-RPC `tools/call` carrying an
736	   invalid payload (e.g. `propose_epics` with `epics: []`); assert an `isError` result and no
737	   `proposal` snapshot change. (Deterministic, unlike prompting the model into invalid input.)
738	
739	Also verify: a `define` spawn without `epicId` still fails `BAD_REQUEST` (unchanged); stack tests
740	green; `pnpm check` green in both repos.
741

file.content:
# Usage ledger

Account draw per story loop, summed from each spawned session's `result` event (fresh input =
`input_tokens` + `cache_creation_input_tokens`). Modeled cost is what the tokens would bill at API
rates; on the Max subscription it is pool draw, not billing.

## 001-01 Session runner & kind registry (2026-07-16 to 2026-07-17)

| Session                        | Model / effort | Fresh input | Output  | Cache reads | Modeled cost |
| ------------------------------ | -------------- | ----------- | ------- | ----------- | ------------ |
| Run 1: implementation          | Fable / high   | 265,633     | 82,332  | 7,393,514   | $16.75       |
| Nested verification spawns (4) | Fable + Sonnet | 50,007      | 929     | 263,452     | $0.90        |
| Review: spec axis              | Sonnet / high  | 59,075      | 14,906  | 427,868     | $0.71        |
| Review: standards axis         | Sonnet / high  | 44,774      | 14,632  | 164,033     | $0.54        |
| Run 2: review fix-up           | Opus / xhigh   | 145,266     | 3,706   | 1,265,714   | $2.17        |
| **Total**                      |                | **564,755** | **116,505** | **9,514,581** | **$21.07** |

The orchestration session that drove the loop is excluded: its usage is not readable from inside
itself.

## 001-02 Board tools: in-process MCP server & proposals (2026-07-17)

| Session                            | Model / effort  | Fresh input | Output      | Cache reads    | Modeled cost |
| ---------------------------------- | --------------- | ----------- | ----------- | -------------- | ------------ |
| Run 1: implementation              | Opus 4.8 / high | 314,758     | 171,390     | 25,054,820     | $19.95       |
| Nested verification spawns (shape) | Fable / high    | 48,229      | 2,158       | 388,285        | $0.73        |
| Review: spec axis                  | Sonnet / high   | 88,373      | 25,948      | 2,749,789      | $1.75        |
| Review: standards axis (2 turns)   | Sonnet / high   | 183,498     | 22,206      | 3,415,474      | $2.46        |
| **Total**                          |                 | **634,858** | **221,702** | **31,608,368** | **$24.89**   |

This loop ran as the 001-02 experiment (Fable plan, Opus build; ledgers in
`usage-experiment-001-02-*.md`), so run 1 and the orchestration are one session: its numbers are
the build-end snapshot, deduped by message id, and the closing loop's own turns after that
snapshot are excluded, same as 001-01's orchestration.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user asked (single message): "let's proceed using helm's own procedure to implement @.helm/board/epics/001-session-foundation/ . Start with the first epic. At the end of the loop, count how much usage it all drew from my account". Intent: dogfood Helm's own documented loop (Ready story → worktree + branch → headless `claude -p` run with the brief as prompt → cold review grading acceptance criteria → human exit) to implement story 001-01 (Session runner & kind registry), the first Ready story of epic 001-session-foundation, and report total account usage drawn by all spawned sessions at the end. Per Helm's spec (review.md), a human decides every review exit — approve/request-changes/discard must be left to the user.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Helm: self-hosted kanban orchestrator wrapping the user's locally authenticated `claude` CLI (Max subscription, no API billing)
   - Session kinds registry: `run` = claude-fable-5 @ high effort, `review` = claude-sonnet-4-6 @ high effort, always cold
   - Headless CLI invocation: `claude -p --output-format stream-json --verbose --model <id> --effort <level> --strict-mcp-config --allowedTools ... --append-system-prompt ...`, prompt via stdin, NDJSON events (system/init carries session_id + model, rate_limit_event, result carries usage/permission_denials/total_cost_usd)
   - CLI version locally is 2.1.177; has first-class `--effort` flag (low/medium/high/xhigh/max)
   - Worktrees: `~/.helm/worktrees/<repo>/<story-id>/`, branch `helm/001-01-session-runner`, sparse checkout (`--no-cone '/*' '!.helm/board'`) excluding board state
   - Board storage: story frontmatter fixed key order id·status·depends·size·branch·gate·sessions·runs; run entries `{ n, session, brief, started, outcome, grades, tokens, minutes }`; orchestrator (me, in this emulation) is the single writer
   - Two-axis review: spec axis (grade acceptance criteria ✓/✗/unclear with evidence, checkbox only on command-proven evidence) and standards axis (repo rules: CLAUDE.md + .claude/playbooks/conventions.md + writing-style.md + code smells), each its own cold session
   - `pnpm check` = `tsc --noEmit && biome check --write --unsafe` (no tests in repo by policy)
   - `link:../stack` dependencies require a `stack` sibling directory (symlinked for worktree); `pnpm generate` (stack generate) needed for `virtual:stack-procedure` types
   - Usage accounting from result events: usage (input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, output_tokens), modelUsage per-model costUSD, plus nested-session usage summed from `~/.claude/projects/<cwd-slug>/*.jsonl` transcripts
   - python3 not on PATH (nix env) — use `node -e` for JSON parsing

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - `.helm/board/epics/001-session-foundation/01-session-runner.md` — the story card; edited three times (orchestrator writes). Final frontmatter:
     ```
     ---
     id: 001-01
     status: review
     depends: []
     branch: helm/001-01-session-runner
     gate: { passed: 2026-07-16T21:49:22Z, brief: 6a0ac2c4d1264bf5, overrides: [] }

content.content:
1	# Session kinds
2	
3	Every `claude` process Helm spawns belongs to one **session kind**, a closed set that fixes the
4	prompt, tool allowlist, model, and context policy for that stage of the loop. The kind is the one
5	place per-stage configuration lives, so "cheap model for read-only chat, frontier model for
6	implementation" and "compact a long run, reseed a stale chat" are table rows, not scattered flags.
7	Helm stays a single fixed workflow; the kinds are its stages, never user-authored steps
8	([vision](../product/vision.md) §Non-goals). The artifact a kind produces is stamped from a template
9	([templates](./templates.md)). The CLI mechanics each kind rides on are in
10	[claude-integration](./claude-integration.md).
11	
12	## Registry
13	
14	| Kind        | Stage                                   | Tools                                          | Model  | Effort | Context               |
15	| ----------- | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------ | ------ | --------------------- |
16	| `init`      | onboard a repo: propose scaffolding      | read-only + `propose_scaffold`                  | Fable  | high   | reseed on stale       |
17	| `shape`     | Shaping: roadmap/feature chat → epics    | read-only + `propose_epics` / `propose_stories` / `raise_decision` | Fable | high | reseed on stale |
18	| `research`  | resolve a shaping decision by investigation | read-only                                    | Sonnet | high   | always cold           |
19	| `define`    | epic → stories                           | read-only + `propose_stories`                   | Fable  | medium | reseed on stale       |
20	| `refine`    | story → brief                            | read-only + `update_brief` / `resolve_question` (+ `contest_flag` during a gate round) | Fable  | medium | reseed on stale       |
21	| `adversary` | ready gate: attack the brief             | read-only + `flag_risk`                         | Fable  | high   | always cold           |
22	| `run`       | implement a Ready story                  | permission preset + `update_card`               | Fable  | medium (adaptive) | compact under pressure |
23	| `review`    | grade + test a finished run              | read-only + test-command Bash                   | Sonnet | high   | cold                  |
24	| `conflict`  | rebase conflict resolution               | worktree tools                                  | Fable  | high   | cold                  |
25	
26	`ask_user` is available to every kind, not runs alone: it is the one primitive for a session to put
27	a question to the user and end its turn (§Interaction). Read-only means the CLI's Read/Grep/Glob
28	plus the kind's board tools, with no Edit or Bash except where a row adds it.
29	
30	Chat kinds (`init`, `shape`, `define`, `refine`) spawn on the user's message and bypass the run
31	queue; every other kind dispatches through it
32	([runs](../product/features/runs.md) §Queue & rate limits).
33	
34	## Model per kind
35	
36	Each kind names a model, passed as `--model`; the rate-limit pool is shared with interactive use,
37	so the cheapest model that does the job is the default ([vision](../product/vision.md) §The
38	constraint that shapes everything). **Sonnet is the floor**: Haiku is never assigned, because a
39	weak proposal or a missed fact costs more rework than the tier saves. Two models cover the
40	registry:
41	
42	- **Sonnet** (`research`, `review`): extraction and verification against something that exists:
43	  narrow factual code lookups, and criteria grading against test evidence for the human who
44	  approves. Each output is re-checked downstream or mechanically checkable, so the floor does the
45	  job. Review is re-tiered when v2 self-grading makes it autonomous
46	  ([roadmap](../product/roadmap.md)).
47	- **Fable** (`shape`, `define`, `refine`, `adversary`, `run`, `conflict`, `init`): every kind
48	  that synthesizes structure or attacks it, plus `conflict`: a rebase that applies cleanly can
49	  still drop one side's intent, no later gate re-reads the merge itself (review grades the
50	  story's criteria, not the other branch's), and the kind is rare enough that the tier costs
51	  nothing. Opus is absent by dominance, not oversight: published effort-curve benchmarks put
52	  Fable at *low* effort above Opus at its highest setting on agentic coding, at roughly half the
53	  per-task cost, because Opus barely improves with added effort while Fable climbs steeply. Any
54	  budget that affords Opus therefore affords a better Fable point. The curves are agentic-coding
55	  benchmarks; reading them onto the chat kinds is extrapolation, re-verified with the other
56	  fast-moving facts below. Opus is the recorded fallback for the synthesis kinds if Fable's
57	  subscription-inclusion terms shift, and the retry model when a run hits Fable's
58	  safety-classifier refusal (security-adjacent stories can false-positive; the CLI's headless
59	  refusal behavior is unspiked).
60	
61	**Effort is the second axis, capped at high.** Models expose reasoning-effort levels (low ·
62	medium · high · xhigh · max; the top levels vary by model): the tier sets the capability ceiling,
63	effort sets how much of it a session spends per turn. max is excluded outright; xhigh is excluded
64	for its latency and context-window burn everywhere except as `run`'s escalation lever (below).
65	A headless spawn sets effort with the `--effort` flag
66	([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Invocation model). Below the cap, each kind sits
67	at the cheapest point that clears its quality bar, weighed by four factors: what checks the
68	output downstream, the kind's token volume, how steep the effort payoff is (Fable climbs with
69	effort; Sonnet's extraction work barely does), and interactive latency (the chat kinds bypass the
70	queue because a person is waiting, so their ceiling is a turn the user will sit through). High
71	goes where output is unchecked, stakes peak, or thoroughness is the product: `adversary`
72	(reasoning-dense, tiny sessions), `shape` (unchecked omissions at the frame, low volume), `init`
73	(one-time), `conflict` (rare, and a failed rebase wastes a queue slot), `research` (tiny volume,
74	and a wrong finding bakes into the epic structure with no later gate re-reading it), and `review`
75	(effort buys tool-call thoroughness, and evidence is review's whole product; a confidently wrong
76	grade miscalibrates the trust the approver puts in every other grade). Medium goes where a
77	stronger stage re-checks the work: `refine`, `define`, and `run` (gate-checked or review-checked,
78	and Fable at medium still clears every Opus setting on the published curves — `run`'s volume made
79	it the obvious extension of the same argument, and a miss is caught by review rather than shipped
80	unchecked). When quality disappoints, the tune-up order is the medium cells first; `run` also
81	escalates itself on evidence of failure (below). One cost to watch: long-run thinking fills the
82	context window sooner, so it raises compaction pressure, the least-verified mechanic
83	([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Context management).
84	
85	**`run`'s effort adapts per story; its model never does.** The registry cell (medium) is the
86	default. One signal moves it, riding an artifact that already exists: a request-changes exit
87	raises the follow-up run to xhigh ([review](../product/features/review.md) §Three exits),
88	reacting to evidence of failure rather than predicting difficulty from a brief. Refine's `trivial`
89	size hint is retired: it existed only to drop a high-default run to medium, which is now the
90	unconditional default, so the hint has nothing left to do. Dynamic model routing is rejected. The
91	stage already classifies the work, so a classifier session would spend pool tokens re-deriving a
92	known label, and its failure mode, a hard story routed to Sonnet, costs a failed run plus rework,
93	the same asymmetry that sets the Sonnet floor.
94	
95	Two resources are easy to conflate. The **rate-limit pool** is priced per model: Fable burns it
96	roughly twice as fast as Opus per token (pool weighting follows per-token price) while finishing
97	a task in fewer tokens. The **context window** burns the same per token on every tier; effort is
98	what fills it faster. Model family names are stable; the exact ids, relative pool burn, effort
99	mechanics, and Fable's subscription-inclusion terms move fast, so re-verify against current docs
100	before building on them ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md)).
101	
102	## Context policies
103	
104	Three policies cover every kind:
105	
106	- **reseed on stale** (chats). A chat resumes by session id across days or weeks. When the
107	  transcript is gone (Claude Code deletes idle ones after `cleanupPeriodDays`), the resume fails
108	  loud and the kind starts a fresh session seeded from the card
109	  ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Invocation model). The user loses transcript
110	  scroll-back, never the artifact: the brief is the product
111	  ([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md)).
112	- **always cold** (`adversary`, `research`, `review`, `conflict`). These kinds never resume. Each starts fresh
113	  and reads the finished artifact with no chat history, which is the point of the adversary pass: a
114	  cold reader catches what the author and the refine chat talked themselves past.
115	- **compact under pressure** (`run`). A run is the one session long enough to exhaust its context
116	  window mid-task. Near the limit the orchestrator compacts it: progress is summarized and the
117	  session resumes with that summary plus the brief reloaded, so the contract survives
118	  ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Context management).
119	
120	## Interaction
121	
122	Any session asks the user a question through `ask_user`: it records the question, the UI renders a
123	quick-reply form with a free-text fallback, and answering resumes the session with the answer. A
124	question arrives one at a time in dependency order and carries Claude's recommended answer, so the
125	user confirms or redirects, and anything the code can settle is read rather than asked
126	([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Grilling). A run that calls it flips the
127	card to Needs input ([runs](../product/features/runs.md) §Needs input);
128	a chat kind renders the question inline in the drawer. This is the one interactive-question path, so
129	a mid-loop decision reaches the user the same way whichever stage raises it
130	([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Board tools).
131

Every `claude` process Helm spawns belongs to one **session kind**, a closed set that fixes the
prompt, tool allowlist, model, and context policy for that stage of the loop. The kind is the one
place per-stage configuration lives, so "cheap model for read-only chat, frontier model for
implementation" and "compact a long run, reseed a stale chat" are table rows, not scattered flags.
Helm stays a single fixed workflow; the kinds are its stages, never user-authored steps
([vision](../product/vision.md) §Non-goals). The artifact a kind produces is stamped from a template
([templates](./templates.md)). The CLI mechanics each kind rides on are in
[claude-integration](./claude-integration.md).

## Registry

| Kind        | Stage                                   | Tools                                          | Model  | Effort | Context               |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------ | ------ | --------------------- |
| `init`      | onboard a repo: propose scaffolding      | read-only + `propose_scaffold`                  | Fable  | high   | reseed on stale       |
| `shape`     | Shaping: roadmap/feature chat → epics    | read-only + `propose_epics` / `propose_stories` / `raise_decision` | Fable | high | reseed on stale |
| `research`  | resolve a shaping decision by investigation | read-only                                    | Sonnet | high   | always cold           |
| `define`    | epic → stories                           | read-only + `propose_stories`                   | Fable  | medium | reseed on stale       |
| `refine`    | story → brief                            | read-only + `update_brief` / `resolve_question` (+ `contest_flag` during a gate round) | Fable  | medium | reseed on stale       |
| `adversary` | ready gate: attack the brief             | read-only + `flag_risk`                         | Fable  | high   | always cold           |
| `run`       | implement a Ready story                  | permission preset + `update_card`               | Fable  | medium (adaptive) | compact under pressure |
| `review`    | grade + test a finished run              | read-only + test-command Bash                   | Sonnet | high   | cold                  |
| `conflict`  | rebase conflict resolution               | worktree tools                                  | Fable  | high   | cold                  |

`ask_user` is available to every kind, not runs alone: it is the one primitive for a session to put
a question to the user and end its turn (§Interaction). Read-only means the CLI's Read/Grep/Glob
plus the kind's board tools, with no Edit or Bash except where a row adds it.

Chat kinds (`init`, `shape`, `define`, `refine`) spawn on the user's message and bypass the run
queue; every other kind dispatches through it
([runs](../product/features/runs.md) §Queue & rate limits).

## Model per kind

so the cheapest model that does the job is the default ([vision](../product/vision.md) §The
constraint that shapes everything). **Sonnet is the floor**: Haiku is never assigned, because a
weak proposal or a missed fact costs more rework than the tier saves. Two models cover the
registry:

- **Sonnet** (`research`, `review`): extraction and verification against something that exists:
  narrow factual code lookups, and criteria grading against test evidence for the human who
  approves. Each output is re-checked downstream or mechanically checkable, so the floor does the
  job. Review is re-tiered when v2 self-grading makes it autonomous
  ([roadmap](../product/roadmap.md)).
- **Fable** (`shape`, `define`, `refine`, `adversary`, `run`, `conflict`, `init`): every kind
  that synthesizes structure or attacks it, plus `conflict`: a rebase that applies cleanly can
  still drop one side's intent, no later gate re-reads the merge itself (review grades the
  story's criteria, not the other branch's), and the kind is rare enough that the tier costs
  nothing. Opus is absent by dominance, not oversight: published effort-curve benchmarks put
  Fable at *low* effort above Opus at its highest setting on agentic coding, at roughly half the
  per-task cost, because Opus barely improves with added effort while Fable climbs steeply. Any
  budget that affords Opus therefore affords a better Fable point. The curves are agentic-coding
  benchmarks; reading them onto the chat kinds is extrapolation, re-verified with the other
  fast-moving facts below. Opus is the recorded fallback for the synthesis kinds if Fable's
  subscription-inclusion terms shift, and the retry model when a run hits Fable's
  safety-classifier refusal (security-adjacent stories can false-positive; the CLI's headless
  refusal behavior is unspiked).

**Effort is the second axis, capped at high.** Models expose reasoning-effort levels (low ·
medium · high · xhigh · max; the top levels vary by model): the tier sets the capability ceiling,
effort sets how much of it a session spends per turn. max is excluded outright; xhigh is excluded
for its latency and context-window burn everywhere except as `run`'s escalation lever (below).
A headless spawn sets effort with the `--effort` flag
([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Invocation model). Below the cap, each kind sits
at the cheapest point that clears its quality bar, weighed by four factors: what checks the
output downstream, the kind's token volume, how steep the effort payoff is (Fable climbs with
effort; Sonnet's extraction work barely does), and interactive latency (the chat kinds bypass the
queue because a person is waiting, so their ceiling is a turn the user will sit through). High
goes where output is unchecked, stakes peak, or thoroughness is the product: `adversary`
(reasoning-dense, tiny sessions), `shape` (unchecked omissions at the frame, low volume), `init`
(one-time), `conflict` (rare, and a failed rebase wastes a queue slot), `research` (tiny volume,
and a wrong finding bakes into the epic structure with no later gate re-reading it), and `review`
(effort buys tool-call thoroughness, and evidence is review's whole product; a confidently wrong
grade miscalibrates the trust the approver puts in every other grade). Medium goes where a
stronger stage re-checks the work: `refine`, `define`, and `run` (gate-checked or review-checked,
and Fable at medium still clears every Opus setting on the published curves — `run`'s volume made
it the obvious extension of the same argument, and a miss is caught by review rather than shipped
unchecked). When quality disappoints, the tune-up order is the medium cells first; `run` also
escalates itself on evidence of failure (below). One cost to watch: long-run thinking fills the
context window sooner, so it raises compaction pressure, the least-verified mechanic
([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Context management).

default. One signal moves it, riding an artifact that already exists: a request-changes exit
raises the follow-up run to xhigh ([review](../product/features/review.md) §Three exits),
reacting to evidence of failure rather than predicting difficulty from a brief. Refine's `trivial`
size hint is retired: it existed only to drop a high-default run to medium, which is now the
unconditional default, so the hint has nothing left to do. Dynamic model routing is rejected. The
stage already classifies the work, so a classifier session would spend pool tokens re-deriving a
known label, and its failure mode, a hard story routed to Sonnet, costs a failed run plus rework,
the same asymmetry that sets the Sonnet floor.

Two resources are easy to conflate. The **rate-limit pool** is priced per model: Fable burns it
roughly twice as fast as Opus per token (pool weighting follows per-token price) while finishing
a task in fewer tokens. The **context window** burns the same per token on every tier; effort is
what fills it faster. Model family names are stable; the exact ids, relative pool burn, effort
mechanics, and Fable's subscription-inclusion terms move fast, so re-verify against current docs
before building on them ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md)).

## Context policies

Three policies cover every kind:

- **reseed on stale** (chats). A chat resumes by session id across days or weeks. When the
  transcript is gone (Claude Code deletes idle ones after `cleanupPeriodDays`), the resume fails
  loud and the kind starts a fresh session seeded from the card
  ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Invocation model). The user loses transcript
  scroll-back, never the artifact: the brief is the product
  ([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md)).
- **always cold** (`adversary`, `research`, `review`, `conflict`). These kinds never resume. Each starts fresh
  and reads the finished artifact with no chat history, which is the point of the adversary pass: a
  cold reader catches what the author and the refine chat talked themselves past.
- **compact under pressure** (`run`). A run is the one session long enough to exhaust its context
  window mid-task. Near the limit the orchestrator compacts it: progress is summarized and the
  session resumes with that summary plus the brief reloaded, so the contract survives
  ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Context management).

## Interaction

Any session asks the user a question through `ask_user`: it records the question, the UI renders a
quick-reply form with a free-text fallback, and answering resumes the session with the answer. A
question arrives one at a time in dependency order and carries Claude's recommended answer, so the
user confirms or redirects, and anything the code can settle is read rather than asked
([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Grilling). A run that calls it flips the
card to Needs input ([runs](../product/features/runs.md) §Needs input);
a chat kind renders the question inline in the drawer. This is the one interactive-question path, so
a mid-loop decision reaches the user the same way whichever stage raises it
([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Board tools).
